title: Installing Your First DrupalReady Template
excerpt: Step-by-step guide to installing any DrupalReady template using Composer, setting up local development, and applying recipes.

content.heading: Prerequisites
content.body:
Before you begin, ensure you have:

- PHP 8.3+ with extensions: gd, xml, mbstring, curl, zip
- Composer 2.x installed globally
- Lando or DDEV for local development
- Git for version control

content.heading: Step 1: Create Your Project
content.body:
Choose a template and run the Composer create-project command:

```
composer create-project drupalready/starter-business my-site
```

Replace `starter-business` with any template slug (e.g., `starter-healthcare`, `starter-legal`).

content.heading: Step 2: Start Local Development
content.body:
Navigate to your project and start the local environment:

```
cd my-site
lando start
```

This spins up PHP, MariaDB, and all required services.

content.heading: Step 3: Install Drupal
content.body:
Install Drupal with the exported configuration:

```
lando drush site:install --existing-config -y
```

content.heading: Step 4: Apply the Base Recipe
content.body:
Apply the template's base recipe to configure content types, views, and modules:

```
lando drush recipe recipes/base
```

You can also apply optional recipes like `recipes/blog` or `recipes/performance`.

content.heading: Next Steps
content.body: Your site is running! Log in at `/user/login` and explore the custom dashboard. From here you can create content, customize the theme, and configure your site.

title: Customizing Your Template's Theme
excerpt: Learn how to change colors, fonts, logos, and layout in your DrupalReady template theme.

content.heading: Theme File Structure
content.body:
Each template theme is located at `web/themes/custom/drupalready_{template}/`. The structure includes:

- `css/base/variables.css` — Colors, fonts, spacing
- `css/components/` — Individual component styles
- `css/layout/` — Page layout rules
- `templates/` — Twig template overrides
- `js/` — JavaScript behaviors

content.heading: Changing Colors
Changing Fonts
content.body: Update the `--font-sans` and `--font-serif` variables in `variables.css`. If using Google Fonts, add the font import to your theme's `.libraries.yml` file.

content.heading: Updating the Logo
content.body: Go to Appearance > Settings in the Drupal admin. Upload your logo file. For code-level changes, replace the `logo.svg` file in your theme's root directory.

content.heading: Overriding Templates
content.body: To customize markup, copy any Twig template from Drupal core or a module into your theme's `templates/` directory with the same filename. Enable Twig debug in development to see which templates are in use.

title: Understanding Drupal Recipes
excerpt: What Drupal Recipes are, how they work in DrupalReady templates, and how to create your own.

content.heading: What Are Recipes?
content.body: Drupal Recipes are a modern way to package and apply configuration. Unlike install profiles, recipes are composable — you can apply multiple recipes to build up your site's functionality incrementally.

content.heading: How DrupalReady Uses Recipes
content.body:
Each template includes a `recipes/` directory with modular recipes:

- `base/` — Core setup: content types, views, roles, permissions
- Optional recipes like `blog/`, `events/`, `portfolio/` add specific features
- `performance/` — Caching, aggregation, and optimization settings

You apply recipes with: `drush recipe recipes/base`

content.heading: Creating Custom Recipes
content.body:
To create your own recipe:

1. Create a new directory under `recipes/`
2. Add a `recipe.yml` file defining modules to install and config to apply
3. Apply with `drush recipe recipes/your-recipe`

content.heading: Recipe vs. Module
content.body: Use a recipe when you need to configure existing modules. Use a custom module when you need new PHP functionality (hooks, plugins, services).

title: Content Editor's Guide
excerpt: How to create, edit, and manage content using the DrupalReady admin dashboard and content forms.

content.heading: The Dashboard
content.body:
After logging in, you'll see the DrupalReady Dashboard at `/admin/dashboard`. It shows:

- Content statistics (published, drafts, this week)
- Quick action buttons to create content
- Recent content list
- Getting started checklist

Use the quick actions to create new content instantly.

content.heading: Creating Content
content.body:
Click any 'Create' button on the dashboard or go to Content > Add Content. Each content type has a simplified form with:

- Clear field labels and descriptions
- Help text explaining what each field does
- Required fields marked with red asterisks
- Collapsed advanced options (SEO, URL, scheduling)

content.heading: Working with Paragraphs
content.body: Paragraphs are flexible content blocks you add to pages. Click 'Add paragraph' and choose from pre-built components like Hero Banner, Card Grid, CTA Section, etc. Each paragraph has its own fields — fill them in and reorder by dragging.

content.heading: Managing Media
content.body: Upload images and files directly in content forms. All media is stored in the Media Library (Content > Media) for easy reuse. Always add alt text to images — the system requires it for accessibility.

content.heading: Publishing
content.body: Click 'Save & Publish' to make content live immediately. Use 'Save as Draft' to save without publishing. If content moderation is enabled, content goes through Draft → Review → Published workflow.

title: Performance Optimization Guide
excerpt: How to optimize your DrupalReady site for speed using the performance recipe, caching, and CDN configuration.

content.heading: Apply the Performance Recipe
content.body:
Every template includes a performance recipe:

```
lando drush recipe recipes/performance
```

This enables page caching, CSS/JS aggregation, lazy loading, and optimized Twig settings.

content.heading: Production Settings
Redis Caching
content.body: For high-traffic sites, enable Redis by uncommenting the Redis configuration in `settings.performance.php`. Install the Redis module with `composer require drupal/redis`.

content.heading: CDN Configuration
content.body: Place Cloudflare or your preferred CDN in front of your site. The performance settings include reverse proxy configuration you can uncomment.

content.heading: Image Optimization
content.body: Templates include the Focal Point module for smart image cropping and the Lazy module for deferred image loading. Use responsive image styles configured in the theme for optimal delivery.

title: Accessibility Best Practices
excerpt: How DrupalReady enforces accessibility and what content editors need to know about WCAG compliance.

content.heading: Built-In Accessibility
content.body:
Every DrupalReady template includes:

- Required alt text on all images with guidance
- Heading order validation (no skipping H2 → H4)
- editoria11y module for real-time accessibility checking
- ARIA landmarks in all theme templates
- Skip navigation links
- Keyboard-navigable admin interface

content.heading: Writing Accessible Content
content.body:
When creating content:

- Always add descriptive alt text to images
- Use headings in order (H2, then H3, then H4)
- Never use H1 in the body — the page title is H1
- Write descriptive link text (avoid 'click here')
- Provide transcripts for audio/video content

content.heading: Using editoria11y
content.body: The editoria11y module shows a small checker icon on every page. Click it to see accessibility issues in your content. Fix any flagged issues before publishing.

content.heading: Color Contrast
content.body: Template themes are designed with WCAG 2.2 AA contrast ratios. If you customize colors, verify contrast using WebAIM's contrast checker at webaim.org/resources/contrastchecker.

title: Deploying Your Site to Production
excerpt: Step-by-step deployment guides for Platform.sh, Pantheon, and traditional hosting providers.

content.heading: Pre-Deployment Checklist
content.body:
Before deploying:

1. Export all configuration: `drush config:export`
2. Apply the performance recipe: `drush recipe recipes/performance`
3. Verify security modules are configured
4. Test all content types and views
5. Run accessibility checks with editoria11y

content.heading: Platform.sh
content.body: Platform.sh has first-class Drupal support. Add the `.platform.app.yaml` configuration, connect your Git repository, and push to deploy. Platform.sh handles PHP, database, Redis, and file storage automatically.

content.heading: Pantheon
content.body: Create a Drupal site on Pantheon, clone the repo, merge your DrupalReady project, and push to the `master` branch. Use Pantheon's dashboard to promote through Dev → Test → Live environments.

content.heading: Traditional Hosting
content.body:
For LAMP/LEMP hosting:

1. Run `composer install --no-dev`
2. Upload to your server
3. Point web root to `web/`
4. Configure database in `settings.php`
5. Run `drush site:install --existing-config`
6. Set up cron for `drush cron`

title: Security Hardening Your Site
excerpt: Security modules and practices included in every DrupalReady template, and how to strengthen them further.

content.heading: Built-In Security
content.body:
Every template includes:

- SecKit — Content Security Policy headers, XSS protection, clickjacking prevention
- Honeypot — Anti-spam protection on all forms
- Config Split — Environment-specific configuration (dev vs. production)
- HSTS headers — Enforces HTTPS connections

content.heading: User Permissions
content.body: Templates include a curated Content Editor role with minimal permissions. Editors can create and edit content but cannot access site configuration, install modules, or manage users. Only administrators have full access.

content.heading: Keeping Updated
content.body:
Run `composer outdated drupal/*` regularly to check for updates. Apply security patches immediately:

```
composer update drupal/core-recommended --with-dependencies
```

content.heading: Additional Hardening
content.body:
For production sites, also consider:

- Two-factor authentication for admin accounts
- Regular automated backups
- Web Application Firewall (Cloudflare, AWS WAF)
- Regular vulnerability scanning
